narrative:
Los futuros de los principales índices estadounidenses abren al alza, recuperando parte de las pérdidas recientes, impulsados por un creciente optimismo sobre una posible resolución negociada del conflicto en Irán. Este avance refleja la expectativa de que se extenderá el alto al fuego actual, con mediadores pakistaníes involucrados y anuncios inminentes, además de posibles acuerdos de cese al fuego entre Israel y Líbano. Esta perspectiva reduce la prima de riesgo geopolítico que había estado presionando a los mercados, aunque la economía global sigue enfrentando un entorno complicado debido a los altos precios de la energía, que mantienen la inflación elevada y el crecimiento económico lento.

En respuesta, las acciones estadounidenses han alcanzado niveles récord, especialmente en sectores como tecnología y transporte, que se benefician de la mejora en el sentimiento y de resultados corporativos sólidos. Sin embargo, los precios del petróleo Brent se mantienen altos, alrededor de 96 dólares por barril, mucho más elevados que antes del conflicto, lo que sigue generando presiones inflacionarias. Los bonos del Tesoro muestran rendimientos estables, reflejando cautela entre los inversionistas, mientras que el oro y la plata suben ligeramente, indicando que persiste cierta incertidumbre. En Asia, los mercados también muestran ganancias, apoyados por datos económicos positivos en China, aunque con señales mixtas en el consumo.

Desde la perspectiva institucional, los grandes fondos y bancos están adoptando una postura equilibrada: mantienen una exposición significativa a las acciones, confiando en que la desescalada en Medio Oriente sostendrá el mercado, pero evitan aumentar riesgos de forma agresiva debido a las condiciones sobrecompradas y a la persistencia de la inflación por los altos costos energéticos. La fortaleza en los resultados empresariales, especialmente en tecnología, ha reforzado esta confianza, pero el aumento generalizado de precios en materias primas obliga a las compañías a ajustar sus costos, lo que limita el potencial de ganancias futuras y genera cautela sobre la evolución económica.

De cara al futuro, los eventos clave que podrían cambiar este escenario incluyen los anuncios oficiales sobre la extensión o el acuerdo definitivo del alto al fuego en Irán, Israel y Líbano, que podrían dar un nuevo impulso a las acciones si confirman la paz. Además, los reportes de ganancias en Estados Unidos esta semana serán cruciales para evaluar la resistencia de la economía ante la incertidumbre geopolítica y la inflación. También se esperan datos económicos relevantes, como las ventas minoristas y los índices de actividad manufacturera, que ayudarán a definir si la economía puede evitar un deterioro mayor. Sin embargo, riesgos como una posible escalada militar inesperada o problemas en las cadenas de suministro podrían revertir el optimismo actual y aumentar la volatilidad en los mercados.
regime:
🏷️ MARKET REGIME: Régimen Transicional / Volátil 🟡

El mercado muestra una fuerte reacción positiva ante noticias de posible desescalada geopolítica, pero con movimientos que ya reflejan estas expectativas, limitando el upside inmediato. Las rupturas alcistas en índices están presentes pero con sobrecompra y señales de agotamiento, mientras que la volatilidad se mantiene elevada por la persistencia de presiones inflacionarias y riesgos económicos. Típicamente, en este contexto los rebotes se compran con cautela y las rupturas pueden fallar o ser efímeras, indicando un ambiente donde la continuidad del movimiento es incierta y la reacción a noticias es mixta. Los traders novatos deben prepararse para oscilaciones amplias y evitar posiciones agresivas hasta que se confirme un régimen más definido.
market_drivers:
● Futuros del S&P 500 suben moderadamente, reflejando optimismo por posible acuerdo de cese al fuego en Medio Oriente y menor aversión al riesgo.  
● TSMC ($TSM) reporta ganancias y guía al alza por fuerte demanda ligada a inteligencia artificial, impulsando sector tecnológico.  
● JB Hunt ($JBHT) sorprende con resultados sólidos y comentarios optimistas sobre el mercado de transporte, favoreciendo acciones de logística.  
● Entain ($ENTAIN) avanza tras reportar volúmenes positivos en Q1 y mantener guía, destacando resiliencia en consumo discrecional.  
● Easy Jet ($EZJ) cae por pérdidas anticipadas y efecto negativo de guerra en Medio Oriente sobre reservas, presionando sector viajes.  
● PPG ($PPG) anuncia aumentos de precios hasta 20% por costos elevados en materias primas, señalando presión inflacionaria persistente.  
● BlackRock mantiene o aumenta posiciones en activos estadounidenses, indicando confianza institucional en la estabilidad del mercado.  
● Posible anuncio inminente de cese al fuego entre Israel y Líbano reduce incertidumbre geopolítica, limitando impacto alcista adicional en acciones.

vital.source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Thursday April 16, 2026
vital.market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are flat-to-up: S&P +9 points/13bp, Dow +23 points/5bp, Nasdaq +87 points/33bp, and the R2 K is flat. The major Eurozone indices are up 10-20bp, with outperformance in autos, industrials, basic resources, chemicals, tech (both semis and software), media, and retail while utilities, energy, telecoms, banks, travel, and staples underperform . Entain is the top stock in the SXXP after earnings while Barry Callebaut and Easy Jet fall on back of their updates. Stocks in Asia mostly saw gains on Thursday (with some exceptions): TPX +1.17%, NKY +2.38%, Hang Seng +1.72%, HSCEI +2.14%, SHCOMP +0.7%, Shenzhen +1.8%, Taiwan TAIEX +1.12%, Korea KOSPI +2.21%, Australia ASX 200 -0.26%, and India is down ~25bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flattish across the curve). The DXY is approximately unchanged. Brent is climbing 150bp to ~$96.40 despite all the optimism about a Middle East resolution. Gold is rising 50bp while silver advances 100bp. Bitcoin is ticking down 20bp.
•
vital.whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – we didn’t see anything that incremental regarding Iran since the Wed close, but the media remains full of optimistic and hopeful rhetoric about a ceasefire extension and/or full deal , with potential announcements arriving soon (in addition, Israel and Lebanon could announce a ceasefire , perhaps as soon as today). Since a resolution to Middle East unrest is increasingly the consensus view, we think this will stop being a major source of upside equity pressure (we fully anticipate a negotiated resolution to the Iran war, but we also don’t think the world is going back to where it stood before fighting broke out , at least not anytime soon. The big price increase announced from PPG last night isn’t an isolated incident – companies across the board will be forced to adjust pricing given what’s happening in commodities, and ~$80-95 is likely to be the “new normal” for Brent vs. ~$60-70 before the war). On the economic data front , China’s Q1/Mar figures were the big overnight news, and while the numbers were net positive (with higher GDP and industrial production vs. weaker retail sales), they aren’t shifting the overall narrative . The big standouts in earnings included TSMC (another robust set of results, increased guidance, and bullish commentary around AI-linked demand) and JBHT (upside on EPS/revenue and mgmt. talked about the US trucking market being at a positive inflection point thanks in large part to a sharp drop in capacity due to recent regulatory changes).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV , and USB ), a press briefing by Hegseth and Caine at the Pentagon (8am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA and NFLX ). In addition, Pakistani officials are in Iran negotiating a ceasefire extension , and there could be news out today regarding this topic (in addition, the leaders of Israel and Lebanon will speak today, and reports suggest a ceasefire could be announced between the two countries).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
•
vital.thinking_about_markets:
How we’re thinking about markets – while it’s still early in the CQ1 reporting season , and the full fallout from the Iran war hasn’t been felt yet in the economy , we’ve been positively surprised by corporate results thus far , especially the “status quo” messaging from bank CEOs (which have the best insight into macro conditions of any industry). In addition, economic activity (growth and inflation) hasn’t been disrupted as much as feared by the war , at least so far. However, with the SPX up ~11%+ on a MTD basis and the index in overbought territory , the time to aggressively buy has come to an end, at least in the immediate term . For the last 1.5 weeks we’ve felt that an Iran détente/deal was much more likely than not , but this is now becoming the consensus view , which means all the “a deal is close”-type of headlines will lose their ability to rally the market . Finally, the economy won’t escape operation Epic Fury without any impact – the effects of the war will be stagflationary , likely (at least) into the summer or fall .
•

( Vital Knowledge ); Spirit Airlines could liquidate as soon as this week as the firm struggles to emerge from bankruptcy following a spike in fuel prices ( CNBC )
• Calendar for Thursday 4/16 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV, TSM, USB ), US weekly jobless claims (8:30am ET), the Philadelphia Fed index for April (8:30am ET), US industrial/manufacturing production for March (9:15am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA, NFLX ).
• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : all signs point to the US and Iran heading toward a sustainable détente , if not a full deal . Reports suggest the US and Iran could extend the current ceasefire for an additional two weeks (to allow more time for negotiations), while Israel contemplates a short-term ceasefire in Lebanon . Iran could permit all ships to sail freely through Hormuz on the Oman side of the waterway according to Reuters (this would both unlock the Strait and allow Iran to say it still “controls” the shipping lane, albeit just one side of it ). Trump seems to be pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation (it’s very likely that the bombing campaign won’t resume, and a large-scale troop deployment is extremely improbable).
• What bears are talking about : Trump remains in an Iran pickle , unable to escalate militarily or fully exit . The blockade could trigger an unexpected military confrontation , causing an immediate resumption of the war (a Washington Post report Wed morning said the Pentagon is still sending additional troops and resources into the Middle East). Iran issued a warning on Wed that it would target Red Sea shipping if the Hormuz blockade persisted . The stagflationary economic fallout from the war , which is only just now seeping into growth and inflation data , will continue for at least the next several months .

International macro

• Australia’s unemployment rate stayed unchanged in Mar at 4.3% , inline w/the Street ( Reuters )
• China reported upside on Q1 GDP (+5% vs. the Street +4.8%) and Mar industrial production (+5.7% vs. the Street +5.4%) while Mar retail sales fell short (+1.7% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Reuters )
• Chinese AI firms could face penalties for illegally copying US models ( Bloomberg )
• ECB likely to leave rates unchanged at the upcoming Apr meeting ( Bloomberg )
• Europe could purchase LNG from Canada’s Pacific coast and ship it via the Panama Canal ( Reuters )
• Geopolitical uncertainty isn’t confined to the Middle East – a USA Today report on Wed said the Pentagon was ramping planning for a possible operation in Cuba.
• Japan’s fin min said an agreement was reached w/the US to intensify communication on FX ( Reuters )
• Norway’s SWF , the world’s largest such fund, doesn’t plan to reduce its holdings of US assets ( FT )
• Saudi Arabia’s massive Public Investment Fund , which has nearly $1 T in assets , will launch a new initiative focused on efficiency following a period of heavy spending (this was discussed in an FT article ); the LIV golf league could be at risk of “imminent closure” as PIF prepares to pull its financial backing for the initiative (this was discussed in a WSJ article ).
• UK economic data for Feb came in ahead of expectations , including GDP (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.1%) and industrial production (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.2%), while manufacturing production fell short (-0.1% vs. the Street +0.3%) ( WSJ )
vital.us_macro_section:
US macro

• Tensions seemed to ease between the US and China on Wed after Trump sounded satisfied with Xi’s denial about Beijing sending weapons to Iran.
• US core inflation is likely to stay ~100bp above the 2% goal for the balance of the year according to St. Louis Fed President Musalem ( Reuters )
• The White House’s pressure campaign on the Fed continues , with Trump warning he will fire Powell if the Fed chief doesn’t step down.
• Tillis will block Warsh’s confirmation , and he could also stand in the way of a Bondi replacement at DOJ , unless the White House drops its Powell/Fed investigation ( Politico )
• The Beige Book encouragingly sent a “status quo” signal about the US economy despite elevated uncertainty from the Iran war (you can find our full takeaways here ).
• Era of the “mega layoff” may have arrived as companies shed thousands of workers at a single time, and get rewarded by the market for it ( WSJ )
• The White House published a fact sheet on Wed outlining the fairly large increase in tax refunds so far this year thanks to the TCJA .
• White House will pressure oil and gas executives to increase production as the administration looks to lower energy costs ( Politico )

Autos

• Auto makers approached by the Pentagon about potentially manufacturing weapons ( WSJ )
• F (Ford) announced a sweeping reorganization that will see its EV business merged into its industrial operation , and Doug Field , the high-profile chief of EV, will be departing the company .

Consumer

• Barry Callebaut shares tumble in European trading after the company cut its profit outlook for the year due to a sharp slump in cocoa bean prices ( Barry Callebaut )
• BF’b (Brown-Forman) received a takeout bid worth $32/shr. from Sazerac ( WSJ )
• Easy Jet shares slide in London trading after the company warned it would post a GBP540-560 M H1 loss due to elevated fuel expense and legal provisions, while the Middle East war weighs on near-term bookings ( Easy Jet )
• Entain shares rally in London after the company reported solid Q1 results (volumes were a bright spot) and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Entain )
• Kering outlined a plan to more than double its 2025 op. margin over the mid-term ( Kering )
• LYV (Live Nation) said the jury verdict on Wed is not the final word on the issue and that aggregate damages would be below $150 MM (which would be trebled). LYV has already accrued $280 MM toward state damages and civil penalty claims.
• Pernod Ricard reported upside on FQ3 organic revenue, with total volumes back to growth at +4%, although mgmt. warns of a neg. impact form the Middle East war , and it now see organic sales -3-4% for the full year ( Pernod Ricard )
• PZZA (Papa John’s) is in talks with Irth Capital about a takeout deal , and an agreement could be in place by the time earnings get reported in early May ( Reuters )
• QVC Group will file for bankruptcy as soon as Wed ( Bloomberg )
• Tesco reported earnings upside but issued a wider guidance range than normal due to uncertainty caused by the Middle East war ( Tesco )
• YUM (Yum) is proceeding with plans to sell Pizza Hut to a PE firm and could soon enter into exclusive talks w/a single buyer ( Reuters )

Financials/Real Estate

• Bank earnings have been strong and encouraging in aggregate – while certain line items fell short (including FICC revenue at GS and BAC), earnings across the board exceeded the Street consensus (Equities Trading has been a notable bright spot), guidance was (at least) reiterated (Bo A tweaked up its NII forecast), and mgmt. teams are signaling a resilient/stable macro backdrop (this was the Bo A macro comment Wed morning – “we remain watchful of evolving risks . However, we saw healthy client activity, including solid consumer spending and stable asset quality , indicating a resilient American economy ”).
• GS (Goldman) was caught offsides on trades tied to interest rates in Q1 , which is why its FICC revenue fell short of expectations ( CNBC , FT )
• SLG (SL Green) reported a shortfall on Q1 FFO (84c vs. the Street consensus of 1.07), but the full-year FFO guidance was reiterated (at 4.40-4.70) and leasing/occupancy was robust . The company signed 929.2 K worth of leases , “the highest volume ever achieved during the first quarter in the company’s history ” and up strongly from 766 K in Q4, with a 16.1% growth in rents (“the highest average starting rent for leases signed in any one quarter in the company’s history”) and a 140bp Q/Q increase in occupancy to 94.4%.

Healthcare

• OGN (Organon) has received takeout interest from India’s Sun Pharma and Germany’s Grunenthal ( Bloomberg )
• QDEL (Quide Ortho) issued a negative preannouncement for Q1 sales ($615-620 MM vs. the Street $678 MM), w/the miss driven by a weaker respiratory season , slower China distributor sales , and delayed EMEA orders linked to the Iran war . For the full-year, mgmt. believes the low-end of its prior guidance ranges are still achievable .

Industrials/Materials/Energy

• ALLE (Allegion) increased its buyback , with $500 M now authorized.
• Daikin shares jumped 9% in Japanese trading after Elliott took a stake and pushed for mgmt. to take steps to bolster shareholder returns ( Reuters )
• Industrial stocks saw selling pressure on Wed in the US due in part to the warning from Canada’s Bombardier Recreational Products , who said the impact of tariffs would be larger than anticipated ( Barron’s )
• JBHT (JB Hunt) reported a few cents of EPS upside in Q1 (1.49 vs. the Street 1.44), and sales were higher too ($3.06 B vs. the Street $2.95 B) as the company executed well and took share in a “dynamic” environment . In the critical Intermodal business, sales and op. income both came in ahead of expectations (“Overall demand for our domestic intermodal service offering in the quarter remained strong, delivering the highest first quarter volume in our company’s history and a record volume week in March . We saw notable strength in our eastern network as service execution and our value proposition continues to drive growth and mode conversion”). On the call, mgmt. said the overall truckload market is improving as regulatory changes remove capacity from the system while demand shows early signs of improvement (“Continued regulatory enforcement to improve safety in our industry has removed non-compliant capacity and, when combined with early signs of improved demand, resulted in a tighter truckload market throughout the quarter. While predicting inflection points is never precise, we believe we are on a path of recovery”). This sanguine JBHT update comes on the heels of the latest Cass monthly freight volume update for March , which came in ahead of expectations and seasonality.
• OC (Owens Corning) is cutting the sales price of its glass reinforcements business (the EV is now $645 MM vs. the prior $755 MM) to reflect changing market conditions .
• PPG (PPG) preannounced upside on Q1 EPS (1.83 vs. the Street 1.71), w/the beat driven by higher prices (volumes were flat) and cost cutting , and the Q2 guide is about inline (they see EPS and organic revenue flat-to-up 3%). In addition to the earnings preannouncement , the company said it would implement price increases of up to 20% across its product lines in response to a significant jump in input costs (which stem from the Iran war). “In recent weeks, the global petrochemical, energy and transportation markets have experienced significant volatility and supply constraints driven by factors outside the control of individual suppliers or manufacturers. This is resulting in higher costs for raw materials, energy, logistics and packaging across the coatings value chain”. A slew of chemical firms have hiked selling prices in recent weeks in response to enormous supply chain challenges related to events in the Middle East , underscoring how the economy is likely to face upward inflation pressure from the war for months to come .

vital.international_macro_section:
International macro

• Australia’s unemployment rate stayed unchanged in Mar at 4.3% , inline w/the Street ( Reuters )
• China reported upside on Q1 GDP (+5% vs. the Street +4.8%) and Mar industrial production (+5.7% vs. the Street +5.4%) while Mar retail sales fell short (+1.7% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Reuters )
• Chinese AI firms could face penalties for illegally copying US models ( Bloomberg )
• ECB likely to leave rates unchanged at the upcoming Apr meeting ( Bloomberg )
• Europe could purchase LNG from Canada’s Pacific coast and ship it via the Panama Canal ( Reuters )
• Geopolitical uncertainty isn’t confined to the Middle East – a USA Today report on Wed said the Pentagon was ramping planning for a possible operation in Cuba.
• Japan’s fin min said an agreement was reached w/the US to intensify communication on FX ( Reuters )
• Norway’s SWF , the world’s largest such fund, doesn’t plan to reduce its holdings of US assets ( FT )
• Saudi Arabia’s massive Public Investment Fund , which has nearly $1 T in assets , will launch a new initiative focused on efficiency following a period of heavy spending (this was discussed in an FT article ); the LIV golf league could be at risk of “imminent closure” as PIF prepares to pull its financial backing for the initiative (this was discussed in a WSJ article ).
• UK economic data for Feb came in ahead of expectations , including GDP (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.1%) and industrial production (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.2%), while manufacturing production fell short (-0.1% vs. the Street +0.3%) ( WSJ )

US macro

• Tensions seemed to ease between the US and China on Wed after Trump sounded satisfied with Xi’s denial about Beijing sending weapons to Iran.
• US core inflation is likely to stay ~100bp above the 2% goal for the balance of the year according to St. Louis Fed President Musalem ( Reuters )
• The White House’s pressure campaign on the Fed continues , with Trump warning he will fire Powell if the Fed chief doesn’t step down.
• Tillis will block Warsh’s confirmation , and he could also stand in the way of a Bondi replacement at DOJ , unless the White House drops its Powell/Fed investigation ( Politico )
• The Beige Book encouragingly sent a “status quo” signal about the US economy despite elevated uncertainty from the Iran war (you can find our full takeaways here ).
• Era of the “mega layoff” may have arrived as companies shed thousands of workers at a single time, and get rewarded by the market for it ( WSJ )
• The White House published a fact sheet on Wed outlining the fairly large increase in tax refunds so far this year thanks to the TCJA .
• White House will pressure oil and gas executives to increase production as the administration looks to lower
vital.energy_section:
energy, telecoms, banks, travel, and staples underperform . Entain is the top stock in the SXXP after earnings while Barry Callebaut and Easy Jet fall on back of their updates. Stocks in Asia mostly saw gains on Thursday (with some exceptions): TPX +1.17%, NKY +2.38%, Hang Seng +1.72%, HSCEI +2.14%, SHCOMP +0.7%, Shenzhen +1.8%, Taiwan TAIEX +1.12%, Korea KOSPI +2.21%, Australia ASX 200 -0.26%, and India is down ~25bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flattish across the curve). The DXY is approximately unchanged. Brent is climbing 150bp to ~$96.40 despite all the optimism about a Middle East resolution. Gold is rising 50bp while silver advances 100bp. Bitcoin is ticking down 20bp.
• What’s happening this morning – we didn’t see anything that incremental regarding Iran since the Wed close, but the media remains full of optimistic and hopeful rhetoric about a ceasefire extension and/or full deal , with potential announcements arriving soon (in addition, Israel and Lebanon could announce a ceasefire , perhaps as soon as today). Since a resolution to Middle East unrest is increasingly the consensus view, we think this will stop being a major source of upside equity pressure (we fully anticipate a negotiated resolution to the Iran war, but we also don’t think the world is going back to where it stood before fighting broke out , at least not anytime soon. The big price increase announced from PPG last night isn’t an isolated incident – companies across the board will be forced to adjust pricing given what’s happening in commodities, and ~$80-95 is likely to be the “new normal” for Brent vs. ~$60-70 before the war). On the economic data front , China’s Q1/Mar figures were the big overnight news, and while the numbers were net positive (with higher GDP and industrial production vs. weaker retail sales), they aren’t shifting the overall narrative . The big standouts in earnings included TSMC (another robust set of results, increased guidance, and bullish commentary around AI-linked demand) and JBHT (upside on EPS/revenue and mgmt. talked about the US trucking market being at a positive inflection point thanks in large part to a sharp drop in capacity due to recent regulatory changes).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV , and USB ), a press briefing by Hegseth and Caine at the Pentagon (8am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA and NFLX ). In addition, Pakistani officials are in Iran negotiating a ceasefire extension , and there could be news out today regarding this topic (in addition, the leaders of Israel and Lebanon will speak today, and reports suggest a ceasefire could be announced between the two countries).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
• How we’re thinking about markets – while it’s still early in the CQ1 reporting season , and the full fallout from the Iran war hasn’t been felt yet in the economy , we’ve been positively surprised by corporate results thus far , especially the “status quo” messaging from bank CEOs (which have the best insight into macro conditions of any industry). In addition, economic activity (growth and inflation) hasn’t been disrupted as much as feared by the war , at least so far. However, with the SPX up ~11%+ on a MTD basis and the index in overbought territory , the time to aggressively buy has come to an end, at least in the immediate term . For the last 1.5 weeks we’ve felt that an Iran détente/deal was much more likely than not , but this is now becoming the consensus view , which means all the “a deal is close”-type of headlines will lose their ability to rally the market . Finally, the economy won’t escape operation Epic Fury without any impact – the effects of the war will be stagflationary , likely (at least) into the summer or fall .
• Market in a Minute (macro) – China reported upside on Q1 GDP (+5% vs. the Street +4.8%) and Mar industrial production (+5.7% vs. the Street +5.4%) while Mar retail sales fell short (+1.7% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Reuters ); Tillis will block Warsh’s confirmation , and he could also stand in the way of a Bondi replacement at DOJ , unless the White House drops its Powell/Fed investigation ( Politico ); White House will pressure oil and gas executives to increase production as the administration looks to lower energy costs ( Politico ); ECB likely to leave rates unchanged at the upcoming Apr meeting ( Bloomberg )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Thursday morning) - Barry Callebaut shares tumble in European trading after the company cut its profit outlook for the year due to a sharp slump in cocoa bean prices ( Barry Callebaut ); Daikin shares jumped 9% in Japanese trading after Elliott took a stake and pushed for mgmt. to take steps to bolster shareholder returns ( Reuters ); Easy Jet shares slide in London trading after the company warned it would post a GBP540-560 M H1 loss due to elevated fuel expense and legal provisions, while the Middle East war weighs on near-term bookings ( Easy Jet ); Entain shares rally in London after the company reported solid Q1 results (volumes were a bright spot) and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Entain ); OGN (Organon) has received takeout interest from India’s Sun Pharma and Germany’s Grunenthal ( Bloomberg ); Pernod Ricard reported upside on FQ3 organic revenue, with total volumes back to growth at +4%, although mgmt. warns of a neg. impact form the Middle East war, and it now see organic sales -3-4% for the full year ( Pernod Ricard ); Tesco reported earnings upside but issued a wider guidance range than normal due to uncertainty caused by the Middle East war ( Tesco ); TSM (TSMC) reported upside on Q1 EPS thanks to robust margins (revenue was previously reported), the Q2 guidance is ahead of expectations, mgmt. said it didn’t expect a near-term impact from Middle East supply chain disruptions, capex for the year is seen at the high-end of the prior guidance range ($52-56 B), the full-year revenue outlook was tweaked higher (now >30% vs. the prior “close to 30%”), and macro commentary was bullish ( Vital Knowledge )

( Vital Knowledge ); Spirit Airlines could liquidate as soon as this week as the firm struggles to emerge from bankruptcy following a spike in fuel prices ( CNBC )
• Calendar for Thursday 4/16 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV, TSM, USB ), US weekly jobless claims (8:30am ET), the Philadelphia Fed index for April (8:30am ET), US industrial/manufacturing production for March (9:15am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA, NFLX ).
• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : all signs point to the US and Iran heading toward a sustainable détente , if not a full deal . Reports suggest the US and Iran could extend the current ceasefire for an additional two weeks (to allow more time for negotiations), while Israel contemplates a short-term ceasefire in Lebanon . Iran could permit all ships to sail freely through Hormuz on the Oman side of the waterway according to Reuters (this would both unlock the Strait and allow Iran to say it still “controls” the shipping lane, albeit just one side of it ). Trump seems to be pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation (it’s very likely that the bombing campaign won’t resume, and a large-scale troop deployment is extremely improbable).
• What bears are talking about : Trump remains in an Iran pickle , unable to escalate militarily or fully exit . The blockade could trigger an unexpected military confrontation , causing an immediate resumption of the war (a Washington Post report Wed morning said the Pentagon is still sending additional troops and resources into the Middle East). Iran issued a warning on Wed that it would target Red Sea shipping if the Hormuz blockade persisted . The stagflationary economic fallout from the war , which is only just now seeping into growth and inflation data , will continue for at least the next several months .

International macro

• Australia’s unemployment rate stayed unchanged in Mar at 4.3% , inline w/the Street ( Reuters )
• China reported upside on Q1 GDP (+5% vs. the Street +4.8%) and Mar industrial production (+5.7% vs. the Street +5.4%) while Mar retail sales fell short (+1.7% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Reuters )
• Chinese AI firms could face penalties for illegally copying US models ( Bloomberg )
• ECB likely to leave rates unchanged at the upcoming Apr meeting ( Bloomberg )
• Europe could purchase LNG from Canada’s Pacific coast and ship it via the Panama Canal ( Reuters )
• Geopolitical uncertainty isn’t confined to the Middle East – a USA Today report on Wed said the Pentagon was ramping planning for a possible operation in Cuba.
• Japan’s fin min said an agreement was reached w/the US to intensify communication on FX ( Reuters )
• Norway’s SWF , the world’s largest such fund, doesn’t plan to reduce its holdings of US assets ( FT )
• Saudi Arabia’s massive Public Investment Fund , which has nearly $1 T in assets , will launch a new initiative focused on efficiency following a period of heavy spending (this was discussed in an FT article ); the LIV golf league could be at risk of “imminent closure” as PIF prepares to pull its financial backing for the initiative (this was discussed in a WSJ article ).
• UK economic data for Feb came in ahead of expectations , including GDP (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.1%) and industrial production (+0.5% vs. the Street +0.2%), while manufacturing production fell short (-0.1% vs. the Street +0.3%) ( WSJ )

US macro

• Tensions seemed to ease between the US and China on Wed after Trump sounded satisfied with Xi’s denial about Beijing sending weapons to Iran.
• US core inflation is likely to stay ~100bp above the 2% goal for the balance of the year according to St. Louis Fed President Musalem ( Reuters )
• The White House’s pressure campaign on the Fed continues , with Trump warning he will fire Powell if the Fed chief doesn’t step down.
• Tillis will block Warsh’s confirmation , and he could also stand in the way of a Bondi replacement at DOJ , unless the White House drops its Powell/Fed investigation ( Politico )
• The Beige Book encouragingly sent a “status quo” signal about the US economy despite elevated uncertainty from the Iran war (you can find our full takeaways here ).
• Era of the “mega layoff” may have arrived as companies shed thousands of workers at a single time, and get rewarded by the market for it ( WSJ )
• The White House published a fact sheet on Wed outlining the fairly large increase in tax refunds so far this year thanks to the TCJA .
• White House will pressure oil and gas executives to increase production as the administration looks to lower energy costs ( Politico )

Autos

• Auto makers approached by the Pentagon about potentially manufacturing weapons ( WSJ )
• F (Ford) announced a sweeping reorganization that will see its EV business merged into its industrial operation , and Doug Field , the high-profile chief of EV, will be departing the company .

Consumer

• Barry Callebaut shares tumble in European trading after the company cut its profit outlook for the year due to a sharp slump in cocoa bean prices ( Barry Callebaut )
• BF’b (Brown-Forman) received a takeout bid worth $32/shr. from Sazerac ( WSJ )
• Easy Jet shares slide in London trading after the company warned it would post a GBP540-560 M H1 loss due to elevated fuel expense and legal provisions, while the Middle East war weighs on near-term bookings ( Easy Jet )
• Entain shares rally in London after the company reported solid Q1 results (volumes were a bright spot) and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Entain )
• Kering outlined a plan to more than double its 2025 op. margin over the mid-term ( Kering )
• LYV (Live Nation) said the jury verdict on Wed is not the final word on the issue and that aggregate damages would be below $150 MM (which would be trebled). LYV has already accrued $280 MM toward state damages and civil penalty claims.
• Pernod Ricard reported upside on FQ3 organic revenue, with total volumes back to growth at +4%, although mgmt. warns of a neg. impact form the Middle East war , and it now see organic sales -3-4% for the full year ( Pernod Ricard )
• PZZA (Papa John’s) is in talks with Irth Capital about a takeout deal , and an agreement could be in place by the time earnings get reported in early May ( Reuters )
• QVC Group will file for bankruptcy as soon as Wed ( Bloomberg )
• Tesco reported earnings upside but issued a wider guidance range than normal due to uncertainty caused by the Middle East war ( Tesco )
• YUM (Yum) is proceeding with plans to sell Pizza Hut to a PE firm and could soon enter into exclusive talks w/a single buyer ( Reuters )

• Chinese AI firms could face penalties for illegally copying US models ( Bloomberg )
• ESE (ESCO Technologies) agreed to acquire the Megger Group Limited (Megger) business of TBG AG (TBG) for $2.35 B ($900 MM in cash and $1.4 B in equity). “Reflecting their confidence in ESCO’s growth and value creation, TBG has agreed to certain lock-up provisions with respect to its equity ownership in ESCO common stock”.
• GOOGL (Google)’s stake in Space X could be worth ~$100 B if the rocket company secures a valuation of $2 T in its upcoming IPO ( Bloomberg )
• Software rally continues – what’s driving it? ( Vital Knowledge )
• Space X/x AI – AI coding startup Cursor will train its latest model using x AI infrastructure ( Business Insider )
• Space X will offer potential anchor investors in its upcoming IPO a tour of facilities in CA and TX ( Bloomberg )
• Space X/Tesla – Musk is calling on the chip equipment industry to move rapidly to help accommodate his Terafab ambitions ( Bloomberg )
• TSM (TSMC) reported upside on Q1 EPS thanks to robust margins (revenue was previously reported), the Q2 guidance is ahead of expectations (including sales of $39-40.2 B vs. the Street $38.1 B, GMs of 66.5% vs. the Street 64.1%), and op. margins of 57.5% vs. the Street 55.3%), mgmt. said it didn’t expect a near-term impact from Middle East supply chain disruptions , capex for the year is seen at the high-end of the prior guidance range ($52-56 B), the full-year revenue outlook was tweaked higher (now >30% vs. the prior “close to 30%”), and macro commentary was bullish (“ AI-related demand continues to be extremely robust . The shift from generative AI and query mode to agentic AI and command and action mode is leading to another stir up in the amount of tokens being consumed . This is driving the need for more and more computation , which supports the robust demand for leading-edge silicon. Our customers continue to provide us with their very strong signal and positive outlook . Thus, our conviction in the multi-year AI megatrend remains high ”).

reuters.source_subject: Hopes for Middle East peace grow
reuters.body_excerpt:
Plus, Trump tries an economic reset.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Kate Turton

Hello. Hopes for a deal to end the Iran war grow, Trump tries an economic reset as Republicans fret over high gas prices, and Russian strikes kill 16 across Ukraine.

Plus, US lawyers say AI chats could be used against you.

Today's Top News

Israeli soldiers near the Israel-Lebanon border. REUTERS/Florion Goga

Middle East

•

Optimism grew that the war in the Middle East may be near an end , with a key Pakistani mediator in Tehran and the administration of US President Donald Trump talking up hopes for a deal that would open the crucial Strait of Hormuz.
•

Trump told ABC News that he's predicting an "amazing two days ahead." White House correspondent Steve Holland tells the Reuters World News podcast that Trump's timeline is likely overly optimistic. Listen now.

United States

• Trump will look to brush off concerns about the economy and the sagging political prospects of his Republican Party during a campaign-style swing through the battleground states of Nevada and Arizona, as the war with Iran pushes gasoline prices higher.
• US Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will highlight nutrition and food safety when he appears before lawmakers, leaving out references to overhauling ‌the vaccination schedule and identifying the causes of autism, his written testimony shows.
•

As people increasingly turn to artificial intelligence for advice, some US lawyers are telling their clients not to treat AI chatbots like trusted confidants when their freedom or legal liability is on the line.

In other news

• Russia unleashed its deadliest attack so far this year on the Ukrainian capital Kyiv and ‌other cities overnight, killing 16 people, including a 12-year-old child, and wounding scores.
•

Pope Leo urged Cameroon's government to root out corruption and resist "the whims of the rich and powerful" , in a forceful speech given in the presence of President Paul Biya, who has led the country since 1982.
•

South African opposition politician Julius Malema was given a five-year prison sentence for firing a rifle in the air at a rally, a magistrate's court decided.

Business & Markets

• The European Union is drafting plans to tackle a looming jet fuel supply crunch and maximise refinery output, officials said. European airlines have warned of jet fuel shortages within weeks as ‌a result of the Iran war, disrupting travel ahead of summer.

• China's economy picked up speed early in 2026, riding an export surge before the Iran war sent energy costs soaring and put global demand - vital to Beijing's growth ambitions - at risk .
•

The prospect of a smooth-and-on-time transition to Trump's pick for the next Federal Reserve chief, Kevin Warsh, looks increasingly to be on shaky ground, setting up a possible clash over who runs things in ‌the meantime.
•

The US nearly turned into a net crude exporter last week for the first time since World War Two as shipments surged close to a record high to meet demand from Asian and European buyers scrambling to replace Middle East supplies.
•
Boeing is hiring around 100 to 140 factory workers a week , the highest pace since 2024, as the US ‌jetmaker replaces retirees and increases staffing to support higher production rates and new models, a union leader said.
•

Economists gathered this week for the IMF and World Bank meetings amid soaring oil prices. In this Viewsroom podcast , Breakingviews columnists discuss how debt-laden countries face an impossible task of managing inflation.

Myanmar refugees seek risky sea routes to safety

Deadly maritime journeys have become a recurring feature of a long-running humanitarian crisis, as refugees from Myanmar continue to risk their lives on overcrowded, unseaworthy boats in search of safety and opportunity.

For years, members of Myanmar’s Rohingya Muslim minority have risked their lives on flimsy wooden boats, driven by violence at home and desperate conditions in crowded refugee camps in Bangladesh, hoping to reach safety and opportunity in countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia or Thailand.

Read our visual story

And Finally...

Steven Spielberg at the Universal Pictures and Focus Features presentation at Cinema Con. Las Vegas, Nevada. REUTERS/Caroline Brehman

Steven Spielberg described his 1977 UFO film "Close Encounters of the Third Kind" as ‌his own "speculation" about intelligent life on other planets.
His new alien movie, "Disclosure Day," will offer what Spielberg believes is "more truth than fiction," the veteran filmmaker told theater operators at the Cinema Con convention in Las Vegas.
